title: Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
subsection content: This test plan targets the post-silicon validation of Socket RAPL functionality at the highest feature hierarchy, focusing on its integration and operation within the SOC. Socket RAPL provides power and frequency limit enforcement across multiple domains (core, memory, etc.), interfacing with BIOS, TPMI, and various IPs. Key flows include BIOS initialization and locking of RAPL limits, TPMI-based domain enumeration and access, propagation of power/frequency ceilings from root to leaf IMHs and CBB dies, aggregation and real-time reporting of performance status, and coordinated enforcement via cross-domain PID controllers. The plan covers interactions between BIOS, TPMI, IMHs, CBB dies, and system-level status reporting, ensuring robust enforcement and accurate system state reflection under all operational and stress scenarios.

title: Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
subsection content: The scope encompasses validation of BIOS and TPMI configuration integrity (including programming, locking, and domain enumeration), propagation and enforcement of power/frequency limits across all IPs, aggregation and reporting of LEAF_PERF_STATUS and RAPL_PERF_STATUS, stress and boundary testing (including negative scenarios and error handling), and cross-domain PID controller interactions. All flows from initialization to runtime enforcement, status reporting, and system response to invalid or extreme configurations are included. Out-of-scope items are any features not directly related to Socket RAPL, such as non-RAPL power management, unrelated BIOS/TPMI features, or IPs outside the RAPL domain.

title: Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
subsection content: All features require access to BIOS configuration interfaces, TPMI registers, CSR access for RAPL limits and lock bits, and system-level monitoring tools for status registers. Hardware must support domain enumeration and real-time status reporting. Stress tools for generating extreme power/frequency demands and error injection capabilities are needed.

title: Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
subsection content: Potential security threats include unauthorized modification of RAPL limits post-lock, improper domain enumeration exposing sensitive configuration, and manipulation of status reporting. Mitigations involve strict enforcement of lock bits, access control on TPMI and CSR interfaces, and validation of error handling for invalid configuration attempts.

title: Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
subsection content: Risks include incomplete coverage of cross-domain interactions, undetected failures in lock enforcement, and inaccurate status aggregation under stress. Contingencies involve additional targeted testing for edge cases, enhanced monitoring during stress scenarios, and fallback to manual validation if automated tools fail.

title: Socket RAPL Feature Validation at SOC Integration Level
subsection content: Prerequisites include a fully functional BIOS with RAPL programming capability, TPMI register access, SOC hardware supporting all RAPL domains, and availability of stress and error injection tools. Initial domain mapping and lock bit configuration must be completed prior to test execution.

title: Cross-Domain PID Controller Interaction
subsection content: Validate that when multiple RAPL domains are enabled, the system enforces the most constraining limit. Test transitions between domains, parallel flows, and coordination between memory and core throttling. Expected outcome: minimum PID output determines frequency ceiling, and all domains interact without conflict.

title: Cross-Domain Minimum PID Output Enforcement
subsection content: Validate that the minimum PID output across all enabled RAPL domains sets the frequency ceiling for all cores and fabrics, and that only one perf limit reason flag is asserted at any time.

title: Cross-Domain Minimum PID Output Enforcement
subsection content: Randomly program PL1 and PL2 limits for Socket, Platform, and DRAM RAPL domains using CSR and TPMI, apply varying workloads, and observe RAPL_PERF_LIMIT HPM messages to verify frequency ceiling and perf limit flag behavior.

title: Cross-Domain Minimum PID Output Enforcement
subsection content: Randomize PL1/PL2 limits for each domain and workload patterns to cover all possible combinations of domain constraints.

title: Cross-Domain Minimum PID Output Enforcement
subsection content: Requires CSR and TPMI interfaces, HPM message monitoring, and ability to sample core/uncore frequencies and perf limit flags.

title: Cross-Domain Minimum PID Output Enforcement
subsection content: Involves Socket RAPL, Platform RAPL, DRAM RAPL, IMH-P, IMH-S, CBBs, and their interaction via HPM messages and frequency ceiling enforcement.

title: Cross-Domain Minimum PID Output Enforcement
subsection content: Must observe 1-hot perf limit flag assertion and verify no domain's power limit is violated; debug via HPM message and frequency sampling.

title: Randomized RAPL Limit Programming and Frequency Ceiling Enforcement
subsection content: Randomly program PL1 and PL2 limits for Socket, Platform, and DRAM RAPL domains using CSR and TPMI interfaces. Apply workloads that drive power consumption above and below the programmed limits for each domain. Continuously monitor RAPL_PERF_LIMIT HPM messages, core and uncore frequencies, and perf limit reason flags. Verify that the minimum PID output across all enabled domains determines the frequency ceiling for all cores and fabrics, and that only one perf limit reason flag is asserted at any time.

title: Randomized RAPL Limit Programming and Frequency Ceiling Enforcement
subsection content: 1. Enable and configure independent PL1 and PL2 limits for Socket, Platform, and DRAM RAPL domains via CSR and TPMI. 2. Randomly select legal values for PL1 and PL2 for each domain and program them. 3. Apply workloads that vary power consumption in each domain above and below their respective limits. 4. Sample RAPL_PERF_LIMIT HPM messages every 500us/1ms and record RAPL_PID_FREQ_OUTPUT and RACL_PID_FREQ_OUTPUT fields. 5. Sample core and uncore frequencies at the same interval. 6. Check that the minimum PID output from all enabled domains matches the frequency ceiling applied to all cores and fabrics. 7. Verify that only one perf limit reason flag is asserted at any time, following the documented priority. 8. Repeat for multiple randomizations to cover all domain combinations.

title: Randomized RAPL Limit Programming and Frequency Ceiling Enforcement
subsection content: Pass if at all times the most constraining (lowest) PID output from any enabled domain sets the frequency ceiling, and only one perf limit reason flag is asserted. Fail if any domain's power limit is exceeded, if frequency ceiling does not match minimum PID output, or if more than one perf limit reason flag is asserted.

title: Randomized RAPL Limit Programming and Frequency Ceiling Enforcement
subsection content: Use Visa/PMON to sample HPM messages, core/uncore frequencies, and perf limit flags. Cross-check logs for frequency ceiling and flag assertion.

title: Randomized RAPL Limit Programming and Frequency Ceiling Enforcement
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. Enable PVT checkers for RAPL domain enforcement and frequency ceiling logic.

title: Randomized RAPL Limit Programming and Frequency Ceiling Enforcement
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: Use OSBV/SVOS (latest validated version), PMON (latest), Perspec path for workload generation, PythonSV for register access, Supercollider for concurrency stress, and Prime95 for synthetic workload.

title: Randomized RAPL Limit Programming and Frequency Ceiling Enforcement
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with RAPL domain enablement and CSR/TPMI access, validated BIOS version, OS kernel with HPM message support, scheduler settings for workload control, and up-to-date PCIe/storage drivers.

title: Randomized RAPL Limit Programming and Frequency Ceiling Enforcement
subsection title: Test HW Requirements and HW Configuration *
subsection content: Platform with SoC supporting Socket, Platform, and DRAM RAPL domains, correct stepping and TDP, full DIMM population, PCIe devices as required, thermal solution per platform spec, debug instrumentation for HPM and frequency sampling, and power measurement setup.

title: Workload-Induced Domain Constraint Rotation
subsection content: Sequentially rotate which RAPL domain is the most constraining by adjusting PL1/PL2 limits and workload focus. For each rotation, verify that the frequency ceiling tracks the domain with the lowest PID output and that only the corresponding perf limit reason flag is asserted.

title: Workload-Induced Domain Constraint Rotation
subsection content: 1. Enable all RAPL domains and set initial PL1/PL2 limits. 2. For each test iteration, lower the PL1/PL2 limit of one domain to make it the most constraining. 3. Apply targeted workload to stress the selected domain. 4. Monitor RAPL_PERF_LIMIT HPM messages and core/uncore frequencies. 5. Verify that the frequency ceiling matches the minimum PID output and that the perf limit reason flag corresponds to the constrained domain. 6. Repeat for each domain.

title: Workload-Induced Domain Constraint Rotation
subsection content: Pass if frequency ceiling always matches the most constraining domain and only the correct perf limit reason flag is asserted. Fail if frequency ceiling does not update or if multiple flags are asserted.

title: Workload-Induced Domain Constraint Rotation
subsection content: Use PMON and Visa to correlate workload, HPM messages, and frequency ceiling. Review logs for correct flag assertion.

title: Workload-Induced Domain Constraint Rotation
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. Use PVT checkers for domain rotation and flag assertion.

title: Workload-Induced Domain Constraint Rotation
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PMON, Perspec path, PythonSV, Supercollider, Prime95.

title: Workload-Induced Domain Constraint Rotation
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with RAPL domain support, validated BIOS version, OS kernel with HPM support, scheduler and driver settings as required.

title: Workload-Induced Domain Constraint Rotation

title: Perf Limit Reason Flag Priority Verification
subsection content: Program multiple RAPL domains with overlapping constraints such that more than one domain could assert a perf limit reason flag. Verify that only one flag is asserted at any time, following the documented priority order: Platform RAPL PL2 > Platform RAPL PL1 > Fast RAPL > Socket RAPL PL2 > Socket RAPL PL1.

title: Perf Limit Reason Flag Priority Verification
subsection content: 1. Enable all RAPL domains and set PL1/PL2 limits to create overlapping constraints. 2. Apply workloads to trigger multiple domains to reach their limits simultaneously. 3. Monitor RAPL_PERF_LIMIT HPM messages and perf limit reason flags. 4. For each event, verify that only one flag is asserted and that it matches the documented priority. 5. Repeat with different combinations of domain constraints.

title: Perf Limit Reason Flag Priority Verification
subsection content: Pass if only one perf limit reason flag is asserted at any time and matches the priority order. Fail if multiple flags are asserted or if the wrong flag is set.

title: Perf Limit Reason Flag Priority Verification
subsection content: Use PMON and Visa to monitor HPM messages and flag assertion. Review logs for correct priority enforcement.

title: Perf Limit Reason Flag Priority Verification
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. Use PVT checkers for flag priority logic.

title: Perf Limit Reason Flag Priority Verification
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PMON, Perspec path, PythonSV, Supercollider, Prime95.

title: Perf Limit Reason Flag Priority Verification
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with RAPL domain support, validated BIOS version, OS kernel with HPM support, scheduler and driver settings as required.

title: Perf Limit Reason Flag Priority Verification

subsection content: SoC with all RAPL domains, full DIMM and PCIe population, platform topology per spec, thermal and debug setup.

title: Domain Transition and Dynamic Limit Reconfiguration
subsection content: Validate that dynamic enable/disable and reconfiguration of RAPL domains and limits results in correct frequency ceiling and perf limit flag updates within 1ms, with no domain exceeding its power limit during transitions.

title: Domain Transition and Dynamic Limit Reconfiguration
subsection content: Randomly enable/disable RAPL domains and reconfigure PL1/PL2 limits at runtime via CSR/TPMI, apply workload transitions, and monitor HPM messages and energy status registers for correct behavior.

title: Domain Transition and Dynamic Limit Reconfiguration
subsection content: Randomize domain enable/disable sequences, PL1/PL2 limit values, and workload types to cover all transition scenarios.

title: Domain Transition and Dynamic Limit Reconfiguration
subsection content: Requires CSR/TPMI interfaces, HPM message and energy status register monitoring, and ability to apply diverse workloads.

title: Domain Transition and Dynamic Limit Reconfiguration
subsection content: Involves Socket, Platform, and DRAM RAPL domains, IMH-P, IMH-S, and their coordination via HPM messages and frequency ceiling enforcement during dynamic transitions.

title: Domain Transition and Dynamic Limit Reconfiguration
subsection content: Must verify frequency ceiling and perf limit flag update within 1ms of configuration change, and no domain exceeds power limit during transitions.

title: Dynamic Domain Enable/Disable and Frequency Ceiling Tracking
subsection content: Randomly enable and disable individual RAPL domains and reconfigure their PL1/PL2 limits at runtime using CSR and TPMI interfaces. Apply workload transitions to stress different domains. Monitor RAPL_PERF_LIMIT HPM messages and verify that the frequency ceiling dynamically tracks the minimum PID output as domains are enabled or disabled and limits are changed.

title: Dynamic Domain Enable/Disable and Frequency Ceiling Tracking
subsection content: 1. Enable multiple RAPL domains and set initial PL1/PL2 limits. 2. Randomly select a domain to disable or enable and reconfigure its limits via CSR/TPMI. 3. Apply a workload transition (e.g., switch from core-intensive to memory-intensive). 4. Monitor RAPL_PERF_LIMIT HPM messages every 500us/1ms and record frequency ceiling and perf limit reason flags. 5. Verify that frequency ceiling and perf limit reason flags update within 1ms of domain configuration change. 6. Repeat for multiple random sequences of domain transitions and limit changes.

title: Dynamic Domain Enable/Disable and Frequency Ceiling Tracking
subsection content: Pass if frequency ceiling and perf limit reason flags update within 1ms of domain configuration change and always reflect the minimum PID output. Fail if update is delayed, incorrect, or if any domain's power limit is violated.

title: Dynamic Domain Enable/Disable and Frequency Ceiling Tracking
subsection content: Use PMON and Visa to monitor HPM messages, frequency ceiling, and perf limit flags. Cross-check logs for update timing and correctness.

title: Dynamic Domain Enable/Disable and Frequency Ceiling Tracking
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. Enable PVT checkers for dynamic domain transitions.

title: Dynamic Domain Enable/Disable and Frequency Ceiling Tracking
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PMON, Perspec path, PythonSV, Supercollider, Prime95.

title: Dynamic Domain Enable/Disable and Frequency Ceiling Tracking
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with RAPL domain enable/disable support, validated BIOS version, OS kernel with HPM and energy status support, scheduler and driver settings as required.

title: Workload Transition Stress and Power Limit Verification
subsection content: Apply random workload transitions (memory-intensive, core-intensive, mixed) while dynamically reconfiguring RAPL domain limits. Monitor energy status registers for each domain to verify that no domain exceeds its programmed power limit during transitions.

title: Workload Transition Stress and Power Limit Verification
subsection content: 1. Enable all RAPL domains and set initial PL1/PL2 limits. 2. Apply a sequence of workload transitions, each time reconfiguring PL1/PL2 limits for one or more domains via CSR/TPMI. 3. Monitor energy status registers for each domain throughout the test. 4. Verify that accumulated energy does not exceed the programmed power limit for any domain during or after transitions. 5. Repeat for multiple random sequences.

title: Workload Transition Stress and Power Limit Verification
subsection content: Pass if no domain exceeds its programmed power limit during any transition. Fail if any domain's energy status indicates a violation.

title: Workload Transition Stress and Power Limit Verification
subsection content: Use PMON and Visa to monitor energy status registers and correlate with workload and limit changes.

title: Configuration Change Latency Measurement
subsection content: Measure the latency between a domain configuration change (enable/disable or limit reprogram) and the update of frequency ceiling and perf limit reason flags in the HPM message. Verify that updates occur within 1ms as required.

title: Configuration Change Latency Measurement
subsection content: 1. Enable all RAPL domains and set initial PL1/PL2 limits. 2. At a known timestamp, change the configuration of a domain (enable/disable or reprogram limit) via CSR/TPMI. 3. Continuously sample HPM messages and record the timestamp when frequency ceiling and perf limit reason flags update. 4. Calculate the latency between configuration change and HPM update. 5. Repeat for multiple domains and configuration changes.

title: Configuration Change Latency Measurement
subsection content: Pass if all updates occur within 1ms of configuration change. Fail if any update exceeds 1ms.

title: Configuration Change Latency Measurement
subsection content: Use PMON and Visa to correlate configuration change events with HPM message updates. Review logs for latency measurement.

title: Configuration Change Latency Measurement
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. Enable PVT checkers for configuration change latency.

title: Configuration Change Latency Measurement
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PMON, Perspec path, PythonSV, Supercollider, Prime95.

title: Configuration Change Latency Measurement
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with RAPL domain support, validated BIOS version, OS kernel with HPM support, scheduler and driver settings as required.

title: Parallel Memory and Core Throttling Coordination
subsection content: Validate that memory and core throttling are coordinated in parallel, with the minimum PID output across Socket and DRAM RAPL domains setting the frequency ceiling for both, and that no domain's power limit is violated.

title: Parallel Memory and Core Throttling Coordination
subsection content: Randomly configure Socket and DRAM RAPL limits, apply mixed workloads to stress both memory and core, and monitor HPM messages and frequency samples for coordinated throttling.

title: Parallel Memory and Core Throttling Coordination
subsection content: Randomize PL1/PL2 limits for Socket and DRAM RAPL domains and workload patterns to cover all possible constraint scenarios.

title: Parallel Memory and Core Throttling Coordination
subsection content: Requires CSR/TPMI interfaces, HPM message and frequency sampling, and ability to apply mixed workloads.

title: Parallel Memory and Core Throttling Coordination
subsection content: Involves Socket RAPL, DRAM RAPL, IMH-P, and their coordination via HPM messages and frequency ceiling enforcement for both memory and core.

title: Parallel Memory and Core Throttling Coordination
subsection content: Must verify parallel throttling and no power limit violation; debug via HPM message and frequency sampling.

title: Coordinated Throttling with Randomized Domain Constraints
subsection content: Randomly configure PL1 and PL2 limits for Socket and DRAM RAPL domains using CSR and TPMI interfaces. Apply mixed workloads that stress both memory and core subsystems. Monitor RAPL_PERF_LIMIT HPM messages, core and memory frequencies, and verify that both memory and core throttling occur in parallel, coordinated by the minimum PID output across domains.

title: Coordinated Throttling with Randomized Domain Constraints
subsection content: 1. Enable and configure independent PL1 and PL2 limits for Socket and DRAM RAPL domains via CSR and TPMI. 2. Randomly select legal values for PL1 and PL2 for each domain and program them. 3. Apply mixed workloads to stress both memory and core. 4. Sample RAPL_PERF_LIMIT HPM messages every 500us/1ms and record minimum PID output. 5. Sample core and memory frequencies at the same interval. 6. Verify that both memory and core frequencies are clipped to the ceiling set by the minimum PID output. 7. Repeat for multiple randomizations.

title: Coordinated Throttling with Randomized Domain Constraints
subsection content: Pass if both memory and core frequencies do not exceed the ceiling set by the minimum PID output and no domain's power limit is violated. Fail if either frequency exceeds the ceiling or if any domain's power limit is violated.

title: Coordinated Throttling with Randomized Domain Constraints
subsection content: Use PMON and Visa to sample HPM messages, core/memory frequencies, and power limits. Cross-check logs for coordinated throttling.

title: Coordinated Throttling with Randomized Domain Constraints
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. Enable PVT checkers for parallel throttling.

subsection content: Apply workload patterns that alternately stress memory and core subsystems, causing the most constraining domain to shift between Socket and DRAM RAPL. Monitor HPM messages and frequency samples to verify that the frequency ceiling for both memory and core always tracks the minimum PID output.

title: Workload-Induced Throttling Coordination Verification
subsection content: 1. Enable Socket and DRAM RAPL domains and set initial PL1/PL2 limits. 2. Apply a memory-intensive workload and monitor HPM messages and frequencies. 3. Switch to a core-intensive workload and repeat monitoring. 4. Alternate workloads to shift the most constraining domain. 5. Verify that frequency ceilings for both memory and core always match the minimum PID output. 6. Repeat for multiple workload transitions.

title: Workload-Induced Throttling Coordination Verification
subsection content: Pass if frequency ceilings for both memory and core always match the minimum PID output during workload transitions. Fail if there is any mismatch or power limit violation.

title: Workload-Induced Throttling Coordination Verification
subsection content: Use PMON and Visa to correlate workload, HPM messages, and frequency ceilings. Review logs for correct coordination.

title: Frequency Ceiling Update Latency under Mixed Workloads
subsection content: Measure the latency of frequency ceiling updates for both memory and core after a workload change that shifts the most constraining domain. Verify that updates occur within 1ms as required.

title: Frequency Ceiling Update Latency under Mixed Workloads
subsection content: 1. Enable Socket and DRAM RAPL domains and set initial PL1/PL2 limits. 2. Apply a workload that stresses one domain, then abruptly switch to a workload that stresses the other domain. 3. Monitor HPM messages and frequency samples, recording the timestamp of workload change and frequency ceiling update. 4. Calculate the latency for both memory and core frequency ceilings to update. 5. Repeat for multiple transitions.

title: Frequency Ceiling Update Latency under Mixed Workloads
subsection content: Pass if frequency ceiling updates for both memory and core occur within 1ms of workload change. Fail if update exceeds 1ms.

title: Frequency Ceiling Update Latency under Mixed Workloads
subsection content: Use PMON and Visa to correlate workload changes with frequency ceiling updates. Review logs for latency measurement.

title: Negative Testing: Locked Interface Modification Attempt
subsection content: Validate that attempts to modify Socket RAPL power limit via in-band agent when CSR interface is locked do not result in any change to RAPL perf status or frequency ceiling.

title: Negative Testing: Locked Interface Modification Attempt
subsection content: Lock CSR interface for Socket RAPL via BIOS, attempt in-band modification, and monitor RAPL perf status and frequency ceiling for changes.

title: Negative Testing: Locked Interface Modification Attempt
subsection content: Not applicable; test is deterministic based on lock state.

title: Negative Testing: Locked Interface Modification Attempt
subsection content: Requires BIOS with CSR lock capability, in-band agent for modification attempt, and monitoring of RAPL perf status and frequency ceiling.

title: Negative Testing: Locked Interface Modification Attempt
subsection content: Involves BIOS/CSR lock, in-band agent, and RAPL perf status/frequency ceiling monitoring.

title: Negative Testing: Locked Interface Modification Attempt
subsection content: Must verify no change in RAPL perf status or frequency ceiling after modification attempt; debug via status and frequency sampling.

title: Negative Testing: Locked Interface Modification Attempt
subsection content: Attempt to modify locked interface and verify no effect.

title: Locked CSR Interface Modification Attempt
subsection content: With the CSR interface for Socket RAPL domain locked by BIOS, attempt to modify the Socket RAPL power limit via in-band agent (MSR or driver) while maintaining a constant workload. Monitor RAPL perf status and frequency ceiling before and after the modification attempt to verify that no change occurs.

title: Locked CSR Interface Modification Attempt
subsection content: 1. Configure BIOS to lock the CSR interface for Socket RAPL domain. 2. Boot system and verify lock bit is set. 3. Apply a constant workload to maintain steady power consumption. 4. Attempt to modify Socket RAPL power limit via in-band agent (MSR or driver). 5. Monitor and record RAPL perf status and frequency ceiling before and after the modification attempt. 6. Compare values to confirm no change.

title: Locked CSR Interface Modification Attempt
subsection content: Pass if no change is observed in RAPL perf status or frequency ceiling after modification attempt. Fail if any change is detected.

title: Locked CSR Interface Modification Attempt
subsection content: Use PMON and Visa to monitor RAPL perf status and frequency ceiling. Review logs for any changes.

title: Locked CSR Interface Modification Attempt
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. Enable PVT checkers for lock enforcement.

title: CSR Lock Bit Verification and Negative Path
subsection content: Verify that the CSR lock bit is correctly set by BIOS and that in-band modification attempts are rejected. Attempt to clear the lock bit via in-band agent and confirm that the lock remains enforced.

title: CSR Lock Bit Verification and Negative Path
subsection content: 1. Configure BIOS to set CSR lock bit for Socket RAPL. 2. Boot system and read lock bit status via register access. 3. Attempt to clear lock bit via in-band agent. 4. Attempt to modify Socket RAPL power limit via in-band agent. 5. Monitor RAPL perf status and frequency ceiling for any changes.

title: CSR Lock Bit Verification and Negative Path
subsection content: Pass if lock bit remains set and no change occurs in RAPL perf status or frequency ceiling. Fail if lock bit is cleared or any change is detected.

title: CSR Lock Bit Verification and Negative Path
subsection content: Use PythonSV and PMON to read lock bit and monitor status/frequency. Review logs for enforcement.

title: CSR Lock Bit Verification and Negative Path
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. Enable PVT checkers for lock enforcement.

title: CSR Lock Bit Verification and Negative Path
subsection content: Tool and Content Requirements: OSBV/SVOS, PMON, Perspec path, PythonSV, Supercollider.

title: CSR Lock Bit Verification and Negative Path
subsection content: Test Requirements: BIOS with CSR lock capability, validated BIOS version, OS kernel with RAPL support, scheduler and driver settings as required.

subsection content: SoC with Socket RAPL domain, platform topology per spec, thermal and debug setup.

title: Negative Testing: Disabled Domain Throttling Attempt
subsection content: Validate that when Socket RAPL domain is disabled, applying workload above typical power limit does not result in throttling or perf status increment.

title: Negative Testing: Disabled Domain Throttling Attempt
subsection content: Disable Socket RAPL domain via PBM_DISABLE, apply workload to exceed typical power limit, and monitor core/uncore frequencies and RAPL perf status for any throttling or status increment.

title: Negative Testing: Disabled Domain Throttling Attempt
subsection content: Not applicable; test is deterministic based on domain disable state.

title: Negative Testing: Disabled Domain Throttling Attempt
subsection content: Requires PBM_DISABLE setting, workload generator, and monitoring of core/uncore frequencies and RAPL perf status.

title: Negative Testing: Disabled Domain Throttling Attempt
subsection content: Involves PBM_DISABLE, core/uncore frequency control, and RAPL perf status monitoring.

title: Negative Testing: Disabled Domain Throttling Attempt
subsection content: Must verify no throttling or perf status increment when domain is disabled; debug via frequency and status sampling.

title: Negative Testing: Disabled Domain Throttling Attempt
subsection content: Attempt to trigger throttling with domain disabled and verify no effect.

title: Disabled Domain Throttling Attempt
subsection content: Disable Socket RAPL domain using PBM_DISABLE setting. Apply workload to drive power consumption above the typical Socket RAPL limit. Monitor core and uncore frequencies and RAPL perf status to verify that no throttling is applied and perf status does not increment.

title: Disabled Domain Throttling Attempt
subsection content: 1. Set PBM_DISABLE to disable Socket RAPL domain. 2. Boot system and verify domain is disabled. 3. Apply workload to exceed typical Socket RAPL power limit. 4. Monitor and record core and uncore frequencies and RAPL perf status throughout the test. 5. Compare values to confirm no throttling or status increment.

title: Disabled Domain Throttling Attempt
subsection content: Pass if no throttling is observed and RAPL perf status remains unchanged. Fail if any throttling or status increment occurs.

title: Disabled Domain Throttling Attempt
subsection content: Use PMON and Visa to monitor frequencies and perf status. Review logs for any changes.

title: Disabled Domain Throttling Attempt
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. Enable PVT checkers for domain disable enforcement.

title: Multiple Workload Levels with Disabled Domain
subsection content: With Socket RAPL domain disabled, apply workloads at different intensity levels (moderate, high, maximum) to attempt to trigger throttling. Monitor core/uncore frequencies and RAPL perf status for any changes.

title: Multiple Workload Levels with Disabled Domain
subsection content: 1. Disable Socket RAPL domain via PBM_DISABLE. 2. For each workload level (moderate, high, maximum): a. Apply workload. b. Monitor and record core/uncore frequencies and RAPL perf status. 3. Compare values for each workload.

title: Multiple Workload Levels with Disabled Domain
subsection content: Pass if no throttling or perf status increment is observed for any workload. Fail if any change is detected.

title: Multiple Workload Levels with Disabled Domain
subsection content: Use PMON and Visa to monitor frequencies and status. Review logs for changes.

title: Multiple Workload Levels with Disabled Domain
subsection content: Enable SST, Central, and Concurrency checkers. Enable PVT checkers for domain disable enforcement.

title: Domain Disable Persistence Verification
subsection content: After disabling Socket RAPL domain, perform a system reset and verify that the domain remains disabled. Apply workload to exceed typical power limit and confirm no throttling or perf status increment occurs.

title: Domain Disable Persistence Verification
subsection content: 1. Disable Socket RAPL domain via PBM_DISABLE. 2. Perform a system reset. 3. Verify that Socket RAPL domain remains disabled. 4. Apply workload to exceed typical power limit. 5. Monitor core/uncore frequencies and RAPL perf status for any changes.

title: Domain Disable Persistence Verification
subsection content: Pass if domain remains disabled after reset and no throttling or status increment occurs. Fail if domain is re-enabled or any change is detected.

title: Domain Disable Persistence Verification
subsection content: Use PMON and Visa to monitor domain status, frequencies, and perf status. Review logs for persistence.
